title: Kenneth Roth

Executive director of [Human Rights Watch](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch")

(born September 23, 1955) is an American attorney, [human rights activist](/wiki/Human_rights_activist "Human rights activist"), and writer. He was the [executive director](/wiki/Executive_director "Executive director") of [Human Rights Watch](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch") (HRW) from 1993 to 2022.
["National Security in a Turbulent World - Yale Law School"](https://law.yale.edu/yls-today/yale-law-school-events/national-security-turbulent-world). *law.yale.edu*. Retrieved May 29, 2019.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American human rights activist (born 1955)

| Kenneth Roth | |
| --- | --- |
| Roth during the [Munich Security Conference](/wiki/Munich_Security_Conference "Munich Security Conference"), 2018 | |
| Born | (1955-09-23) September 23, 1955 (age 70)  [Elmhurst, Illinois](/wiki/Elmhurst,_Illinois "Elmhurst, Illinois"), U.S. |
| Education | [Brown University](/wiki/Brown_University "Brown University") ([BA](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Arts "Bachelor of Arts")) [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University") ([JD](/wiki/Juris_Doctor "Juris Doctor")) |
| Known for | Executive director of [Human Rights Watch](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch") |
| Spouse | Annie Sparrow  ​ (m. 2011)​ |

**Kenneth Roth** (born September 23, 1955) is an American attorney, [human rights activist](/wiki/Human_rights_activist "Human rights activist"), and writer. He was the [executive director](/wiki/Executive_director "Executive director") of [Human Rights Watch](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch") (HRW) from 1993 to 2022.

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kenneth_Roth&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Kenneth Roth was born on September 23, 1955, in [Elmhurst, Illinois](/wiki/Elmhurst,_Illinois "Elmhurst, Illinois"), to Muriel T. Roth and Walter S. Roth. His father was a [Jewish refugee](/wiki/Jewish_refugees_from_German-occupied_Europe_in_the_United_Kingdom "Jewish refugees from German-occupied Europe in the United Kingdom") who fled [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany").[[1]](#cite_note-brownalumnimagazine-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) Walter's family had a butchery in [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") near [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt") when [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") [came to power](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler%27s_rise_to_power "Adolf Hitler's rise to power").

Kenneth Roth grew up in [Deerfield, Illinois](/wiki/Deerfield,_Illinois "Deerfield, Illinois").[[4]](#cite_note-nytimes_bio-4) He graduated from [Brown University](/wiki/Brown_University "Brown University") in 1977 with a [BA](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Arts "Bachelor of Arts") in history[[1]](#cite_note-brownalumnimagazine-1) and received his Juris Doctor from [Yale Law School](/wiki/Yale_Law_School "Yale Law School") in 1980.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

On June 13, 2011, Roth was married in an [Anglican](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") church to Annie Sparrow.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kenneth_Roth&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Msc2012_20120205_004_Roth_SZwez.jpg/250px-Msc2012_20120205_004_Roth_SZwez.jpg)](/wiki/File:Msc2012_20120205_004_Roth_SZwez.jpg)

Roth in 2012

Roth worked in private practice as a litigator and served as an [Assistant United States Attorney](/wiki/Assistant_United_States_Attorney "Assistant United States Attorney") for the [United States Department of Justice](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice "United States Department of Justice") for the [Southern District of New York](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_Southern_District_of_New_York "United States District Court for the Southern District of New York") and the [Iran-Contra](/wiki/Iran-Contra "Iran-Contra") investigation in Washington DC.[[6]](#cite_note-winnipeg-6) Roth's career in human rights began inauspiciously. The one human rights course offered at Yale Law School was repeatedly canceled, and upon graduation he found that jobs in the field were few. As a result, as he worked as a lawyer, and volunteered nights and weekends doing human rights work, focusing on[[7]](#cite_note-7) the [Soviet imposition of martial law in Poland](/wiki/Martial_law_in_Poland "Martial law in Poland") in 1981.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Roth joined Human Rights Watch (HRW) in 1987 as deputy director. His initial work centered on Haiti[[9]](#cite_note-9) and gradually extended to Cuba[[10]](#cite_note-10) and the Middle East,[[11]](#cite_note-11) among other places.

Since 1993 (when [Aryeh Neier](/wiki/Aryeh_Neier "Aryeh Neier") left to become head of [George Soros](/wiki/George_Soros "George Soros")'s [Open Society Institute](/wiki/Open_Society_Institute "Open Society Institute")), Roth became the executive director[[6]](#cite_note-winnipeg-6) of HRW. While he was in the office, the HRW staff increased from 60 to 552; HRW shared the Nobel Peace Prize in 1997 for banning of [anti-personnel mines](/wiki/Anti-personnel_mine "Anti-personnel mine"), helped to establish [International Criminal Court](/wiki/International_Criminal_Court "International Criminal Court") and ban [children in the military](/wiki/Children_in_the_military "Children in the military"). Roth resigned from HRW on 31 August 2022.[[12]](#cite_note-softpowernews-12) After leaving HRW, Roth said he intended to write a book.[[4]](#cite_note-nytimes_bio-4)

Roth received honorary degrees from Brown University,[[13]](#cite_note-13) [Bowdoin College](/wiki/Bowdoin_College "Bowdoin College"),[[14]](#cite_note-14) the [University of Ottawa](/wiki/University_of_Ottawa "University of Ottawa"),[[15]](#cite_note-15) and the [American University of Paris](/wiki/American_University_of_Paris "American University of Paris").[[16]](#cite_note-16) He was a recipient of the Athens Democracy Award,[[17]](#cite_note-17) the William Rogers Award[[18]](#cite_note-18) from Brown University and the Jean Mayer Global Citizenship Award[[19]](#cite_note-19) from [Tufts University](/wiki/Tufts_University "Tufts University"). He serves on the [Watson Institute](/wiki/Watson_Institute_for_International_and_Public_Affairs "Watson Institute for International and Public Affairs") Board of Overseers[[20]](#cite_note-20) at Brown University, the Board of Governors of [Bard College Berlin](/wiki/Bard_College_Berlin "Bard College Berlin"),[[21]](#cite_note-21) and the Humanitarian and Development Advisory Panel of the [Novo Nordisk Foundation](/wiki/Novo_Nordisk_Foundation "Novo Nordisk Foundation").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Harvard Kennedy School fellowship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kenneth_Roth&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Harvard Kennedy School fellowship")]

In 2021, Roth was offered a fellowship at the [Harvard Kennedy School](/wiki/Harvard_Kennedy_School "Harvard Kennedy School")'s [Carr Center for Human Rights Policy](/wiki/Carr_Center_for_Human_Rights_Policy "Carr Center for Human Rights Policy"). The offer was withdrawn in 2023. Roth said that the withdrawal was because of his criticism of Israel.[[23]](#cite_note-23) [Michael Massing](/wiki/Michael_Massing "Michael Massing") reported Roth's claims in *[the Nation](/wiki/The_Nation "The Nation")*.[[24]](#cite_note-Nation-24) Roth told [the *Guardian*](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian") and [Amy Goodman](/wiki/Amy_Goodman "Amy Goodman") on *[Democracy Now](/wiki/Democracy_Now! "Democracy Now!")* that he believed Harvard Kennedy School dean [Douglas Elmendorf](/wiki/Douglas_Elmendorf "Douglas Elmendorf") had capitulated to Harvard's donors who are strong supporters of Israel. Kennedy school professor [Kathryn Sikkink](/wiki/Kathryn_Sikkink "Kathryn Sikkink") said she was told that Elmendorf thought Human Rights Watch has an "anti-Israel bias" and that Roth’s tweets on Israel were concerning, points she disputed with the dean.[[24]](#cite_note-Nation-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) Following Roth's complaint, the [ACLU](/wiki/ACLU "ACLU"), [Pen America](/wiki/PEN_America "PEN America"), and other human rights activists condemned the Kennedy School's decision.[[27]](#cite_note-27) On January 10, 2023, HRW published a letter to Harvard President [Lawrence Bacow](/wiki/Lawrence_Bacow "Lawrence Bacow") that said it was concerned about a "lasting impact on scholars and activists, particularly Palestinians, who should not have to fear professional repercussions from Harvard University or another institution if they write or speak critically about the Israeli government".[[28]](#cite_note-28) Following those reports, hundreds of Harvard affiliates called on Elmendorf to resign.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

On January 19, 2023, The Kennedy School, which denies Roth's allegations,[[30]](#cite_note-30) reversed its decision, reoffering Roth the fellowship.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Elmendorf said that his initial decision had been in "error" and was not intended "to limit debate at the Kennedy School about human rights in any country".[[32]](#cite_note-32) Roth responded that Elmendorf failed to say anything to identify the people "who matter to him" who he said were behind his original veto decision. "Full transparency is key to ensuring that such influence is not exerted in other cases", Roth said, adding: "Secondly, I remain worried about academic freedom. Given my three decades leading Human Rights Watch, I was able to shine an intense spotlight on Dean Elmendorf’s decision, but what about others? The problem of people penalized for criticizing Israel is not limited to me."[[33]](#cite_note-33)

In the fall of 2023, Roth joined the [Princeton School of Public and International Affairs](/wiki/Princeton_School_of_Public_and_International_Affairs "Princeton School of Public and International Affairs") as a lecturer and the Charles and Marie Robertson Visiting Professor.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

## Denial of entry to Egypt and China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kenneth_Roth&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Denial of entry to Egypt and China")]

In 2014, the Egyptian government blocked Roth from entering the country.[[35]](#cite_note-35) He was traveling to Egypt to release a report on its government's [August 2013 Rabaa massacre](/wiki/August_2013_Rabaa_massacre "August 2013 Rabaa massacre")[[36]](#cite_note-36) of 817 sit-in protesters.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

In December 2019, China announced unspecified [sanctions](/wiki/Chinese_sanctions "Chinese sanctions") against HRW and several other NGOs because of links to the [Hong Kong democracy movement](/wiki/Hong_Kong_democracy_movement "Hong Kong democracy movement").[[38]](#cite_note-38) In January 2020, Roth said that he was denied entry to [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong").[[39]](#cite_note-39) He was planning to launch the organization's World Report,[[40]](#cite_note-40) which had an essay saying China is a growing threat to human rights around the world.[[41]](#cite_note-41) [Geng Shuang](/wiki/Geng_Shuang "Geng Shuang"), a spokesman for China's foreign ministry, told reporters he would not read the report, accusing Human Rights Watch of distorting the truth and claiming China's human rights situation is “the best it's been in history.”[[42]](#cite_note-42) In August 2020, the Chinese government announced that it had imposed unspecified “sanctions” on Roth.[[43]](#cite_note-43)
